subject: Correction: T3 politician example contains unsourced factual claim — replace with fair witness framing

body.issue: The politician connection example in T3 states: 'Constituent inquiries about ICESCR ratification have increased across SFRC member offices since January 2026.' This reads as a factual assertion but has no source. Congressional offices do not publish incoming contact statistics. No public data supports this claim. The example text was illustrative draft, not verified fact — but as written it would violate fair witness discipline and the substantiation standard if published.
body.scope: This correction applies to ALL example connection texts in T3 and to any similar unsourced claims across T1 and T2. Treat all example text in T1-T3 as illustrative drafts requiring substantiation before publication. If a claim cannot find a source, reframe it as the gap it represents.
body.replacement_approach: Option 3 — acknowledge the data gap explicitly. When the published post needs to reference constituent contact patterns, use this framing pattern instead:
body.replacement_text: Congressional offices do not publish incoming contact statistics, so no public data tracks how many constituents have called about ICESCR ratification. What Congressional Management Foundation research does show: volume of specific asks on a named policy — not generic 'support human rights' messages — creates institutional attention in congressional offices regardless of the topic (CMF, Communicating with Congress). The data gap itself matters: the absence of public tracking means constituent contact on ICESCR operates in a low-visibility space where each call carries disproportionate weight precisely because so few people make them.
body.editorial_rule: For all posts in this series and the March 6 update: when no public data exists for a claim, do not assert the claim and do not silently omit it. Instead, name the gap, cite what adjacent evidence does exist (CMF for contact methodology, congress.gov for co-sponsorship and hearing records, OHCHR for ratification status), and explain why the gap matters. This turns missing data into a fair witness observation rather than a hidden weakness.

available: Congressional Management Foundation — Communicating with Congress (general findings on constituent contact effectiveness)
congress.gov — committee membership, hearing schedules, co-sponsorship records, floor statements
OHCHR treaty body — ratification status, periodic review schedules, concluding observations
Senate Foreign Relations Committee — foreign.senate.gov — current membership
not_available: Incoming constituent contact volumes by topic (congressional offices do not publish)
Internal staff briefing priorities (not public record)
SFRC scheduling deliberations (not public until announced)

claims.text: Congressional offices do not publish incoming constituent contact statistics by topic.
claims.confidence_basis: General knowledge of congressional operations. CMF research describes aggregate patterns but individual office contact logs remain internal.

epistemic_flags: This correction catches an error in the psychology-agent's own output — the T3 example text should have carried an '[illustrative — requires substantiation]' tag from the start
